thai: ข้าวผัดกะเพราหมึก

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai basil squid rice; the broader 'rice dish' term (FOODON_00002809) is used as the closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Thai basil squid rice; A0CKP (stir-fried rice dish with seafood) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite dish; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of basil squid rice weighs approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 200 g steamed white rice and 250 g stir-fried squid with basil sauce, as commonly served at Singapore hawker stalls and Thai restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Squid, mixed species, raw (FDC ID 175168) and Cooked white rice, long-grain (FDC ID 169756), combined with HPB Singapore nutrient reference for composite Thai stir-fry dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai basil squid rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for raw squid (FDC ID 175168), cooked long-grain white rice (FDC ID 169756), and vegetable oil, adjusted for stir-fry cooking losses and seasoning additions (fish sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce) consistent with HPB Singapore guidance for Thai stir-fry rice dishes. Input per_100g values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were reviewed against this composite estimate and found broadly consistent; they are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white rice, boiled (GI 72)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai basil squid rice as a composite dish. The GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component — steamed white rice (long-grain, boiled) — which has a well-established GI of 72 (glucose reference) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The squid and basil stir-fry component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the overall GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated using the rice-fraction carbohydrate: rice is approximately 44% of the 450 g plate (200 g), effective carbohydrate from rice ≈ 31.8 g per serving, giving GL = 72 × 31.8 / 100 = 22.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g from multiple high-sodium condiments (fish sauce, soy sauce, oyster sauce); a standard 450 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, limit portion size to half a standard plate, and increase vegetable content to boost potassium intake and partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice is the dominant carbohydrate source with a GI of 72 (high) and low dietary fibre (0.9 g per 100 g), resulting in rapid postprandial glucose elevation; a standard 450 g serving delivers approximately 80 g total carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 23.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute brown rice or cauliflower rice to lower the glycemic index, reduce the rice portion to 100–150 g, and request reduced oyster sauce and soy sauce to limit added sugars and sodium.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg per 100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is relatively low at 1.1 g per 100 g, and squid provides lean protein with some omega-3 fatty acids, partially offsetting cardiovascular risk; cholesterol is 62.2 mg per 100 g, which is moderate.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce sodium intake, request sauce reduction, and note that squid's lean protein and omega-3 content are beneficial, but the high-sodium condiment profile requires active management to keep total daily sodium within the 1500–2000 mg recommended range.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Holy basil (eugenol, polyphenols) and squid (omega-3 fatty acids) provide anti-inflammatory benefit; however, sodium at 400 mg per 100 g and white rice (high glycaemic load driving systemic inflammation) are concerns; nightshade vegetables (chilli, bell pepper) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory properties of holy basil and squid but should request brown rice, reduced condiment sodium, and monitor individual response to nightshade vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Basil Squid Rice (九层塔苏东配米饭; Thai: ข้าวผัดกะเพราหมึก, romanized: Khao Phat Kaphrao Mueuk) is a Thai-origin stir-fried dish widely served at Singapore hawker stalls and Thai restaurants. It consists of tender squid (sotong) wok-fried over high heat with holy basil leaves, garlic, fresh chilli, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, served over a bed of steamed white rice. The dish delivers a bold, savoury, and mildly spicy flavour profile with the distinctive anise-like fragrance of holy basil. The squid is characteristically tender-chewy when correctly cooked. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 151 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 5.6 g fat (1.1 g saturated), and 17.8 g carbohydrate with only 0.9 g dietary fibre. Sodium is high at 400 mg per 100 g due to the multi-condiment sauce base. A standard 450 g plate delivers approximately 680 kcal and 1800 mg sodium. Clinically, the dish is high-concern for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes due to its sodium load and high-GI white rice base (GI 72). Cardiovascular concern is moderate; saturated fat is low and squid provides lean protein with omega-3 fatty acids. Holy basil contains eugenol and polyphenols with documented anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker contexts. Common modifications include substituting brown rice, reducing sauce volume, and adding extra vegetables to improve the fibre and potassium profile.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai basil squid rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (squid, white rice, oil) cross-referenced against HPB Singapore guidance for Thai stir-fry rice dishes. The input ai_generated values (confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. Medium confidence reflects the estimation basis rather than a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central (squid FDC ID 175168; cooked long-grain white rice FDC ID 169756) combined with stir-fry cooking adjustments and condiment additions, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Thai stir-fry dish guidance. No exact approved-source match exists for this composite dish. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for this composite Thai stir-fry rice dish is not directly available in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB Singapore database at the required specificity as of 2026-04-16: monounsaturated_fat_g (fatty acid fractions not reported for composite stir-fry dishes in approved sources), polyunsaturated_fat_g (same reason), potassium_mg (not reported in HPB Singapore composite dish entries; USDA component data not aggregated for this preparation), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (fat-soluble vitamin data not available for this composite preparation in approved sources), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (B-vitamin profile not reported for this composite dish in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (same reason), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (same reason), vitamin_b6_mg (same reason), vitamin_b12_mcg (same reason), folate_mcg_dfe (same reason), vitamin_d_mcg (fat-soluble vitamin not reported for this composite preparation in approved sources), vitamin_e_mg (same reason), phosphorus_mg (mineral data beyond calcium and iron not reported for this composite dish in approved sources), magnesium_mg (same reason), zinc_mg (same reason), selenium_mcg (same reason), water_g (moisture data for this composite stir-fry rice dish not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database at the required specificity). 3. GI of 72 assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component (steamed white rice, long-grain boiled) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). 4. Halal status reflects common Singapore hawker context; individual vendor certification should be verified. 5. The input ethnic_tags included 'Curry' and 'Pad Thai' which are incorrect for this dish; these have been removed. The dish is a Thai basil stir-fry (pad kra pao), not a curry or pad thai. 6. Tamil local name provided as a transliteration approximation; native Tamil speaker review recommended. 7. FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as the closest defensible parent anchor for stir-fried rice dish with seafood; exact FoodEx2 code for Thai basil squid rice does not exist in the published FoodEx2 catalogue. FoodOn FOODON_00002809 used as closest parent anchor; no exact FoodOn term exists for this specific dish.
